entry.title: Why I Ditched My DIY ReAct Loop for LangGraph (And What I Lost in the Trade)
entry.content:
Introduction​

At some point when building a multi-agent system, you hit a wall. Your hand-rolled orchestration loop works fine in the demo. Then you add streaming. Then session resumption. Then multi-agent handoffs. Then a circuit breaker for runaway LLM loops.

Each feature requires rewriting the same state management logic at a slightly lower level. By the time I had all of these in Aura — my local-first AI shell — the ReactiveOrchestrator class was a 300-line tangle of mutable state, MAX_LOOPS counters, and carefully ordered if statements.

I rewrote it using LangGraph. This post is an honest accounting of what I gained, what the migration actually looked like, and one thing I genuinely lost.

What the DIY Loop Looked Like​

Aura's original orchestration model was a reactive blackboard: every user message, agent output, and tool result gets appended to an immutable event log stored in SQLite. Agents read the full log and "bid" for control by returning a confidence score. The highest bidder executes.

The ReactiveOrchestrator class ran this in a while loop:

// Simplified from the original ReactiveOrchestrator.ts
const MAX_LOOPS = 6;

while (loopCount < MAX_LOOPS) {
  const bids = this.agents.map(a => a.evaluate(events));
  const winner = pickWinner(bids);

  if (!winner || winner.confidence === 0) break;

  const result = await winner.execute(events, bid);
  events.push(result); // append to blackboard

  if (result.event_type === 'synthesis_complete') break;
  if (result.event_type === 'escalation_required') break;

  loopCount++;
}

This worked. Until I needed three things simultaneously:

  1. SQLite-backed checkpointing so sessions survive app restarts
  2. Streaming — sending token-by-token output to the UI as it generates
  3. Multi-agent handoffs where research output feeds code output feeds synthesis

All three require you to reason carefully about when state is written, what is persisted, and how partial results flow between agents. In a while loop, those concerns have no natural home. You end up patching in ad-hoc flags and writing increasingly defensive if chains.

Why LangGraph​

LangGraph models your orchestration as a directed graph: nodes are async functions (agents, tools, compaction passes), edges are routing decisions. State is a typed schema with declared reducers for each field.

The critical insight is that state persistence and streaming become infrastructure concerns, not application concerns. You plug in a checkpointer (SQLite, in our case) and LangGraph handles serialization, resume logic, and partial-state hydration automatically.

Here's what Aura's graph definition looks like now:

export const workflow = new StateGraph(AuraStateSchema)
  .addNode('orchestrator', orchestratorNode)
  .addNode('research_agent', specialistNode)
  .addNode('code_agent', specialistNode)
  .addNode('synthesis_agent', synthesisNode)
  .addNode('compaction', compactionNode)
  .addEdge(START, 'orchestrator');

workflow.addConditionalEdges('orchestrator', (state) => {
  // Circuit breaker: 3 consecutive errors → force synthesis fallback
  if (state.errorCount >= 3) return 'synthesis_agent';
  return state.activeAgent === 'end' ? END : state.activeAgent;
});

workflow.addEdge('research_agent', 'orchestrator');
workflow.addEdge('code_agent', 'orchestrator');
workflow.addEdge('synthesis_agent', 'compaction');
workflow.addEdge('compaction', END);

export const compiledGraph = workflow.compile({
  checkpointer: new SqliteSaver(db), // same db instance as the rest of the app
});

Every loop iteration is now a graph traversal. The circuit breaker is a conditional edge. Session resumption is one line. The loop logic is in the graph structure, not in imperative code.

The State Schema: Memory Separation by Design​

The most consequential design decision in the migration was how to separate memory. The old blackboard was a single flat array. The new schema has explicit zones with different persistence semantics:

export const AuraStateSchema = Annotation.Root({
  // PERSISTENT: user queries + final synthesized answers only
  // Survives session restarts. Reducer concatenates across turns.
  chatHistory: Annotation<BaseMessage[]>({
    reducer: (state, update) => state.concat(update),
    default: () => [],
  }),

  // EPHEMERAL: agent scratchpad — reasoning traces, tool outputs, ReAct steps
  // Explicitly wiped by the compaction node after each turn.
  taskWorkspace: Annotation<BaseMessage[]>({
    reducer: (state, update) => update, // overwrite, not concat
    default: () => [],
  }),

  // TELEMETRY: consecutive error count for the circuit breaker
  errorCount: Annotation<number>({
    reducer: (state, update) => state + update,
    default: () => 0,
  }),
});

The taskWorkspace overwrite reducer is the key move. Intermediate reasoning — tool call results, ReAct [think] / [act] / [observe] traces, pseudo-vector context injections — accumulates during a turn and then gets wiped by the compaction node. Only the clean final answer goes into chatHistory.

This solves context rot: the phenomenon where, over many turns, the LLM context window fills up with stale scratchpad content from previous sessions, degrading response quality and increasing token costs.

The Compaction Node​

The compaction node is a pattern I hadn't seen written about much but turned out to be essential. It runs after synthesis — not before — and does two things:

  1. Extracts durable facts from the ephemeral workspace using a cheap, fast model call
  2. Wipes taskWorkspace clean for the next turn
async function compactionNode(state: typeof AuraStateSchema.State) {
  const workspaceText = state.taskWorkspace.map(m => m.content).join('\n');

  if (workspaceText.length > 100) {
    const prompt = `Review the following agent scratchpad and extract any newly discovered,
permanent facts about the user, their system, or project conventions.
If nothing notable, reply exactly with "NONE".

Scratchpad:
${workspaceText.substring(0, 8000)}`;

    const result = await registry.call(resolveModel('compaction'), prompt, { temperature: 0.1 });

    if (result.text && !result.text.includes('NONE')) {
      // Persist to the USER.md memory file
      await toolRegistry.execute({
        name: 'write_memory',
        arguments: { file: 'USER', content: `Session facts:\n${result.text.trim()}` }
      });
    }
  }

  // Wipe the scratchpad
  return { taskWorkspace: [] };
}

The compaction model call uses the cheapest available model (resolveModel('compaction') maps to Gemini Flash or a Groq fast model). It runs after every turn but only writes to long-term memory when it finds something worth keeping. This gives Aura genuine cross-session memory without accumulating garbage.

Preserving the Blackboard Heuristics​

Here's the part that took the most work. The old ReactiveOrchestrator had battle-tested bid evaluation logic: keyword boosts for research and code queries, a synthesis guard that prevented the synthesis agent from "cutting in" before a specialist had run, and a circuit breaker.

LangGraph didn't replace any of that — it just needed to host it. The orchestratorNode does the same bid evaluation as before, ported verbatim. The key addition is the synthesis guard:

// In orchestratorNode — runs on every loop iteration
const synthIdx = bids.findIndex(b => b.agentName === 'synthesis_agent');
if (synthIdx !== -1) {
  const specialistOutputExists = currentTurnEvents.some(
    e => (e.event_type === 'agent_output' || e.event_type === 'code_written')
      && e.author !== 'synthesis_agent'
  );
  const specialistBiddingNow = bids.some(
    b => b.agentName !== 'synthesis_agent' && b.confidence >= 0.30
  );

  // Synthesis can only win if: a specialist already ran AND no specialist is competing
  const allowMode1 = synthBid.confidence >= 0.85 && specialistOutputExists && !specialistBiddingNow;
  // OR: it's a genuine greeting (hello, who are you, etc.)
  const allowMode2 = synthBid.confidence < 0.85 && isSynthesisGreeting(lastUserMsg);

  if (!allowMode1 && !allowMode2) {
    bids[synthIdx] = { ...synthBid, confidence: 0 }; // suppress synthesis
  }
}

Without the synthesis guard, the synthesis agent — which has broad keyword coverage — would constantly outbid specialists and produce shallow responses that skipped the actual research or code generation step. The guard enforces turn ordering: specialists run first, synthesis runs last.

The Bridge: stateToEvents​

The migration wasn't a full rewrite of the agents themselves. Each agent still implements the same ReactiveAgent interface — evaluate(events) and execute(events, bid) — using the legacy BlackboardEvent[] format.

To keep agents working unchanged, I wrote a bridge function that converts LangGraph's BaseMessage[] state back into the legacy event format:

function stateToEvents(chatHistory: BaseMessage[], workspace: BaseMessage[]): BlackboardEvent[] {
  const events: BlackboardEvent[] = [];

  for (const msg of [...chatHistory, ...workspace]) {
    if (msg._getType() === 'human') {
      events.push({ event_type: 'user_message', author: 'user', content: msg.content, ... });
    } else if (msg._getType() === 'ai') {
      let author = 'synthesis_agent';
      // Map agent prefixes back to the correct author
      if (content.startsWith('[code_agent]'))     { author = 'code_agent'; }
      if (content.startsWith('[research_agent]')) { author = 'research_agent'; }
      // ...
      events.push({ event_type: 'agent_output', author, content, ... });
    }
  }
  return events;
}

This is a deliberate seam. Agent logic and orchestration logic are now cleanly separated. If I want to rewrite an agent to use native LangGraph state directly, I can do that incrementally without touching the graph structure.

What I Lost​

One thing the DIY loop did naturally: fine-grained control over when the loop terminates.

In the old orchestrator, an agent could signal synthesis_complete and the loop would immediately stop — no further bids, no additional nodes. In the LangGraph version, the graph structure determines termination. The synthesis node always routes to compaction, and compaction always routes to END. That's the right architecture, but it means one extra LLM call (compaction) happens on every single turn, even trivial ones.

For a greeter response — "Hello! I'm Aura" — the compaction node still spins up, reads the workspace (empty), finds nothing to extract, and exits. It's a fast no-op, but it's there.

The fix would be a conditional edge from synthesis_agent that skips compaction when the workspace is empty. I haven't added it yet because the overhead is negligible at current usage. But it's a real cost that didn't exist in the DIY loop.

Key Takeaways​

Use LangGraph when you need:

  • Session resumption across restarts (SQLite checkpointer, one line)
  • Streaming to a UI without managing partial state manually
  • Multi-agent handoffs with clear state separation
  • A circuit breaker that's structural, not imperative

Keep your own abstractions when:

  • Your agents have well-tested evaluation heuristics — port them, don't replace them
  • You want incremental migration — the bridge pattern (stateToEvents) lets agents run unchanged

The compaction node is underrated. Persistent memory + workspace wipe after every turn is the practical solution to context rot in long-running agents. Use a cheap model. Run it on every turn. Only write when there's something worth keeping.

The synthesis guard is not optional. Any multi-agent system where one agent synthesizes the output of others needs an explicit rule preventing the synthesis agent from winning before the specialists have run. Without it, you get fast but shallow responses every time.

Conclusion​

The migration from ReactiveOrchestrator to LangGraph was about two weeks of evenings. The agents didn't change. The blackboard heuristics didn't change. What changed was that session persistence, streaming, and loop control stopped being things I had to maintain and became things the framework handles.

If you're at the point where your orchestration loop has its own bugs, that's the signal. The DIY loop is a prototype. LangGraph is infrastructure.

Built with: LangGraph · TypeScript · SQLite (SqliteSaver) · Electron · Vitest

entry.title: Building a Local-First AI Shell from Scratch: Architecture Lessons from Aura
entry.content:
Introduction​

Most AI tooling assumes you're comfortable with your data living in the cloud. Most productivity apps are built for neurotypical, always-online knowledge workers. I built Aura because I'm neither of those things, and I was tired of tools that weren't built for me.

Aura is a local-first, multi-agent AI shell — an Electron + React desktop app that runs multiple specialized LLM agents on your machine, persists state in SQLite, and routes work through a ReAct orchestration loop built on LangGraph. Seven LLM providers. Neubrutalist editorial UI. Zero cloud dependency for core functionality.

This post walks through the architectural decisions I made, what broke, and what I'd do differently.

Background: The Problem with "Smart" AI Apps​

When you open most AI chat apps, you get a single conversation box. You talk to one model. It doesn't remember your projects. It doesn't know what you were doing yesterday. And every session starts completely cold.

For developers — especially those of us with ADHD — this is a brutal UX pattern. Context-switching already fragments executive function. An AI tool that also makes you re-explain your entire codebase every session is actively hostile.

I wanted something different: an agent that holds your project state, knows which provider is cheapest for which task, persists everything locally, and has a UI designed around cognitive load reduction, not feature count.

The Solution: A Multi-Agent Reactive Shell​

Aura's architecture revolves around three core concepts:

1. The Blackboard Model​

Instead of a simple request/response cycle, Aura uses a blackboard event system. Every user message, agent output, tool result, and error is appended to an immutable event log stored in SQLite (blackboard_events table). Agents read the full log and bid for control.

export interface ReactiveAgent {
  readonly name: AgentName;

  // Synchronous — reads events, returns a confidence bid.
  // Never calls an LLM at this stage.
  evaluate(events: BlackboardEvent[]): AgentBid;

  // Async — actually executes and returns output.
  // The orchestrator writes results back to the blackboard.
  execute(events: BlackboardEvent[], bid: AgentBid): Promise<AgentOutput>;
}

This separation is intentional. The evaluate phase is pure — no LLM calls, no side effects. It's just keyword heuristics and event-count checks. Only the winning agent's execute runs asynchronously. This keeps the bid loop fast and debuggable.

2. The Agent Roster​

Aura currently ships four agents:

  • Supervisor — Routes work, chooses the best specialist, maintains task structure. Critically: it never exposes its routing reasoning to the user.
  • Research Agent — Retrieves and summarizes facts from context and tools. Never invents certainty.
  • Code Agent — Explains, generates, and patches code. Prefers small, safe diffs.
  • Synthesis Agent — Produces the final user-facing answer. Strips out all internal orchestration artifacts before responding.

The Synthesis Agent was the last to be added and turned out to be the most important. Without it, users were seeing [research_agent] internal tags and raw ReAct trace fragments leaking into responses. The Synthesis Agent is a dedicated "cleanup pass" that takes the full event log and produces a clean, readable final answer.

3. LangGraph for Orchestration​

I originally rolled my own ReAct loop. It worked fine until I needed checkpointing, streaming, and multi-agent handoffs simultaneously. Three features, three different re-inventions of state machine logic.

Switching to LangGraph solved this cleanly. The graph-based workflow lets me define nodes (agents/tools) and edges (routing conditions) declaratively:

// src/lib/graph/workflow.ts
const workflow = new StateGraph(AuraStateAnnotation)
  .addNode("supervisor", supervisorNode)
  .addNode("research", researchNode)
  .addNode("code", codeNode)
  .addNode("synthesis", synthesisNode)
  .addEdge(START, "supervisor")
  .addConditionalEdges("supervisor", routeFromSupervisor)
  .addEdge("research", "synthesis")
  .addEdge("code", "synthesis")
  .addEdge("synthesis", END);

export const compiledGraph = workflow.compile({
  checkpointer: new SqliteSaver(db),
});

SQLite-backed checkpointing means sessions are resumable across restarts. This was essential for the "never lose context" requirement.

The UI: Neubrutalist + ADHD-Calibrated​

The original Aura UI was Tailwind utility classes everywhere. It worked, but it felt like every other React app. After the neubrutalist redesign — Fraunces serif headings, JetBrains Mono for data, thick borders, high contrast — the app started feeling like mine.

More importantly, I added five UX features specifically designed around ADHD:

Phase 5: ADHD-Calibrated UX Wins
  ✅ Energy Toggle (Low/High) as data-energy on <html>
  ✅ Brain Dump Mode — vague paragraph → auto-decomposed checklist
  ✅ Stream ReAct trace as discrete think/act/observe rows
  ✅ Session resumption badges in NavigationHub
  ✅ Keyboard-first density mode (Tab, Esc, /, g r shortcuts)

Brain Dump Mode deserves its own post. The core insight: with ADHD, the hardest moment isn't doing a task — it's decomposing "I need to do the thing" into actual steps. Brain Dump Mode accepts a raw, vague paragraph ("need to fix the auth flow and also update the readme and check why tests are failing") and uses an agent pass to decompose it into a structured checklist. It externalizes the executive function step that ADHD makes hardest.

Energy Toggle is simpler but surprisingly effective. Low-energy mode dims non-essential UI, increases font sizes, and reduces motion. High-energy mode enables the full interface. One keypress. No settings menu to navigate.

The Multi-Provider Setup​

Aura supports seven LLM providers: Anthropic, Google (Gemini), Groq, OpenRouter, Mistral, Cohere, DeepSeek. The ProviderRegistry manages them behind a unified UnifiedCaller interface:

// You call one method. The registry handles provider selection.
const result = await registry.call({
  messages,
  model: "gemini-2.0-flash",   // or "claude-sonnet-4-6", "llama-3.3-70b", etc.
  tools: [...],
  stream: true,
});

API keys are stored locally in a .env.local file. Docker secrets are supported for containerized deployments. Nothing hits the network except the provider API calls themselves.

What Broke (And What I Learned)​

better-sqlite3 vs Electron vs Node.js v22​

This one cost me an entire day. better-sqlite3 is a native Node.js module — it compiles to a .node binary that's ABI-specific. Electron uses its own Node.js ABI. Tests running under system Node.js v22 can't load the same binary that Electron built.

The fix: run tests inside Docker (where Node version is pinned to match the build environment), and rebuild the module specifically for Electron with electron-rebuild.

# In package.json scripts:
"rebuild:sqlite": "electron-rebuild -f -w better-sqlite3"

Lesson: native modules in Electron are always a layered versioning problem. Plan for it upfront. Pin your Node version in CI.

The Synthesis Guard​

Early Aura builds had a subtle bug where the supervisor's internal routing reasoning (routing_decision: research_agent, confidence: 0.87) was leaking into user-facing responses. This happened because the final node was writing whatever was in state.lastAgentOutput directly to the UI.

The fix was adding an explicit synthesis guard in the orchestrator node — keyword checks that catch and strip internal tags before the response reaches the renderer:

// In workflow.ts orchestratorNode
const INTERNAL_TAG_PATTERN = /\[(supervisor|research_agent|code_agent)\]/gi;
const cleanedOutput = rawOutput.replace(INTERNAL_TAG_PATTERN, '').trim();

Plus the dedicated Synthesis Agent, which now owns the final pass.

Key Takeaways​

  • Separate bid from execute — keep agent selection synchronous and pure, agent execution async. This makes the loop debuggable and fast.
  • LangGraph > DIY ReAct when you need checkpointing + streaming + multi-agent state. The abstraction cost is worth it.
  • The Synthesis Agent is not optional — without a dedicated cleanup pass, internal orchestration artifacts leak to users.
  • Native modules in Electron require explicit rebuild steps — don't fight it, automate it.
  • ADHD-calibrated UX is universal UX — Energy Toggle, Brain Dump, keyboard shortcuts make the app better for everyone, not just neurodivergent users.

Conclusion​

Aura is still in active development. The next milestones are the Veto Layer (tiered authorization so agents can't take destructive actions without confirmation), Firecrawl integration for live web access, and automating my Etsy product listing pipeline.

The architecture is solid. The UI feels right. And for the first time, I have an AI tool that doesn't make me feel like I'm fighting it.

If you're building something similar — local-first, multi-agent, ADHD-aware — I'd love to compare notes. Find me in the repo.

Built with: Electron · React · TypeScript · LangGraph · SQLite · Radix UI · Zustand · Vitest · Docker

entry.title: Setting Up This Blog with Docusaurus and GitHub Pages
entry.summary: I wanted a place to write. Not a newsletter, not a Twitter thread — just a quiet corner of the internet where I can think out loud about things I'm building and learning.
entry.content:
I wanted a place to write. Not a newsletter, not a Twitter thread — just a quiet corner of the internet where I can think out loud about things I'm building and learning.

This post is about how I set up this blog: the stack I chose, the few gotchas I ran into, and why the whole thing took less than an afternoon.

Why Docusaurus?​

The requirements were simple: write in Markdown, deploy to GitHub Pages for free, and not spend two weekends configuring webpack. I looked at a few options — Jekyll, Hugo, Astro, 11ty — and landed on Docusaurus for a few reasons:

  1. It's maintained by Meta's open source team and has active development
  2. It supports MDX out of the box, so I can drop React components into posts if I ever want to
  3. Blog-only mode means I get a clean blog without the docs sidebar I don't need
  4. It handles RSS/Atom feeds, reading-time estimates, and author profiles without plugins

Scaffolding the Project​

npx create-docusaurus@latest my-blog classic
cd my-blog
npm install

The classic preset gives you docs + blog. Since I only want the blog, I needed to turn off the docs section and make the blog serve from the root path.

Blog-Only Mode​

In docusaurus.config.js, inside the presets array, change the blog plugin config:

presets: [
  [
    'classic',
    {
      docs: false,  // disable the docs plugin entirely
      blog: {
        routeBasePath: '/',  // serve blog from the site root
        showReadingTime: true,
        feedOptions: {
          type: ['rss', 'atom'],
          xslt: true,
        },
        onInlineTags: 'warn',
        onInlineAuthors: 'warn',
        onUntruncatedBlogPosts: 'warn',
      },
      theme: {
        customCss: './src/css/custom.css',
      },
    },
  ],
],

With docs: false and routeBasePath: '/', the homepage is now your blog listing. Clean.

GitHub Pages Configuration​

Also worth adding trailingSlash: false explicitly — without it you'll see a warning about inconsistent trailing slash behavior during deployment, which can cause issues with some hosting setups:

trailingSlash: false,

Authors and Tags​

Docusaurus supports a structured authors file. Create blog/authors.yml:

abood:
  name: 0soAbood
  image_url: https://github.com/YourUsername.png
  socials:
    github: YourUsername

And blog/tags.yml for consistent tag metadata:

general:
  label: General
  permalink: /general
  description: General posts

Then in any post's frontmatter:

---
title: "My Post Title"
date: 2026-05-03
authors: abood
tags: [general]
---

Dark Mode​

One line in themeConfig:

themeConfig: {
  colorMode: {
    respectPrefersColorScheme: true,
  },
  // ...
}

This makes the blog respect the user's OS-level dark/light preference automatically. No toggle required.

The Result​

The whole setup — scaffolding, configuration, writing the first post, and getting it ready to deploy — took a few hours. The project structure is minimal:

my-blog/
├── blog/
│   ├── authors.yml
│   ├── tags.yml
│   └── 2026-05-03-first-post.mdx
├── src/
│   └── css/
│       └── custom.css
├── static/
│   └── img/
└── docusaurus.config.js

No database, no CMS, no server. Just Markdown files committed to a repo that deploys automatically via GitHub Actions.

Key Takeaways​

  • Set docs: false and routeBasePath: '/' to get a blog-only Docusaurus site
  • Always update the url, organizationName, and projectName fields in the config before deploying
  • Add trailingSlash: false to avoid GitHub Pages redirect inconsistencies
  • authors.yml and tags.yml keep your post metadata consistent across posts
  • respectPrefersColorScheme: true gives you free dark mode with zero extra work

What's Next​

Now that the plumbing is in place, I can focus on writing. I have a few topics already queued up. We'll see what makes it out of my notes and onto the page.

If you're thinking about starting a blog with a similar setup, the Docusaurus blog documentation is genuinely good — worth a read before you start.

entry.title: Hello, World
entry.summary: First post. Not much to say yet — I'm still figuring out what this place is going to be.
entry.content:
First post. Not much to say yet — I'm still figuring out what this place is going to be.

The plan is to write about things I find interesting as I come across them. No specific topic, no schedule, just stuff worth putting into words.

If you're reading this somehow: hi.
